title: Technical Notes
text:
Here, I collect ideas and comments on research work.


Optimal shapelets tree for time series interpretable classification (2024)

Improvement: As defined in Eq. (1), the distance between a shapelet and a time series is computed by minimizing the distance between the shapelet and all subsequences of the time series having the same length. In the case of long shapelets, the magnitude of the distance may increase with the shapelet length, leading to larger Big-M values in Eqs. (60)–(61) and, consequently, to weaker linear relaxations.
A possible alternative is to use the normalized \(\ell_1\) distance
\[
\operatorname{dist}(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y})
=
\min_{1 \leq p \leq q}
\frac{1}{\ell}
\left\lVert \mathbf{y} - \mathbf{x}_p^\ell \right\rVert_1 .
\]
In this way, we can rewrite Eq. (73) as
\[
M_i^L
=
\max
\left\{
\frac{1}{\ell}
\sum_{j\in[\ell]}
\left(\theta_{ij}^+ + \theta_{ij}^- \right)
-
b
\right\}
\leq
\max
\left\{
\frac{1}{\ell}
\sum_{j\in[\ell]}
|s_j-u_{ij}|
\right\}
\leq
1,
\qquad \forall i\in[n].
\]
Moreover, the threshold variables can be bounded by replacing Eq. (71) with \[
0 \leq b_t \leq 1,
\qquad \forall t\in \mathcal{T}_B.
\]
Similarly, for Eq. (74), we obtain
\[
M_i^R
=
\max\{b\}
=
1,
\qquad \forall i\in[n].
\]



Learning Sparse-Lets for Interpretable Classification of Event-interval Sequences (2024)

Improvement: After being sampled, e-lets/sparse-lets are matched to each e-sequence using a sliding window. Therefore, the length of each e-let/sparse-let must be less than or equal to the length of the shortest e-sequence:
\[
\ell(\mathcal{S}_i^{k, l}) \leq \min_{\mathcal{S}_j \in \mathcal{D}} \, \ell(\mathcal{S}_j), \qquad \forall k \in \{0, \dotsc, \ell(\mathcal{S}_i) - 1\}, \; \forall l \in \{1, \dotsc, \ell(\mathcal{S}_i) - k\}, \; \forall i \in \{1, \dotsc, N\}.
\]
This can be a limitation in applications where e-sequence lengths vary substantially. In such cases, the sampled e-lets/sparse-lets may be unable to capture relevant patterns in the data. One possible solution is to add a “no-event” symbol to the alphabet of the e-sequences. This makes it possible to pad shorter e-sequences with no-event symbols, thereby removing the constraint on the maximum length of e-lets/sparse-lets.

title: Lorenzo Bonasera
text:
I am currently a postdoctoral researcher at DTU Management, working on the DECIDE project with David Pisinger. I obtained my PhD in operations research from the University of Pavia and the University of Lugano (USI), under the supervision of Stefano Gualandi. I previously worked as a research scientist at the German Aerospace Center (DLR) in the Institute for AI Safety and Security, applying Generative AI methods to enhance situational awareness in the maritime domain.
My main research agenda focuses on reducing the arbitrariness in data-driven decision making. I pursue this goal through two complementary research directions:

developing interpretable machine learning models;
designing decision-support systems that account for predictive and prescriptive multiplicity.

You can reach me at: lobon [at] dtu [dot] dk

title: Can quantum-mechanical description of physical reality be considered complete?
text:
Abstract
In a complete theory there is an element corresponding to each element of reality. A sufficient condition for the reality of a physical quantity is the possibility of predicting it with certainty, without disturbing the system. In quantum mechanics in the case of two physical quantities described by non-commuting operators, the knowledge of one precludes the knowledge of the other. Then either (1) the description of reality given by the wave function in quantum mechanics is not complete or (2) these two quantities cannot have simultaneous reality. Consideration of the problem of making predictions concerning a system on the basis of measurements made on another system that had previously interacted with it leads to the result that if (1) is false then (2) is also false. One is thus led to conclude that the description of reality as given by a wave function is not complete.

title: Can quantum-mechanical description of physical reality be considered complete?
